The arena where the Lewiston Maineiacs played their home games can seat how many people?
A: []
A: The Lewiston Maineiacs played their home games at the Androscoggin Bank Colisée (also known as the Lewiston, Maine Colisée) in Lewiston, Maine, United States.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The Lewiston Maineiacs played their home games at the Androscoggin Bank Colisée (also known as the Lewiston, Maine Colisée) in Lewiston, Maine, United States. It can seat 5,398 people.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The Lewiston Maineiacs played their home games at the Androscoggin Bank Colisée (also known as the Lewiston, Maine Colisée) in Lewiston, Maine, United States. It can seat 5,398 people. Therefore the answer is 5,398.
A: []
Q: The arena where the Lewiston Maineiacs played their home games can seat how many people?
A: "The Lewiston Maineiacs played their home games at the Androscoggin Bank Colis\u00e9e (also known as the Lewiston, Maine Colis\u00e9e) in Lewiston, Maine, United States. The Colis\u00e9e's seating capacity is approximately 3,100. The Maineiacs played there until the team moved to the Quebec Major Junior Hockey League in 2008."
A: The Lewiston Maineiacs played their home games at the Androscoggin Bank Colis\u00e9e (also known as the Lewiston, Maine Colis\u00e9e) in Lewiston, Maine, United States. The Colis\u00e9e's seating capacity is approximately 3,100. The Maineiacs played there until the team moved to the Quebec Major Junior Hockey League in 2008

Q: Aside from the Apple Remote, what other device can control the program Apple Remote was originally designed to interact with?
A: "Apple Remote is originally designed to interact with the iTunes program on an iPod. iTunes was a media player and was often used with other media players besides the iPod, such as a digital picture frame, but not a \"device\".  Thus another device would be the iPhone, in addition to iPod. Apple Remote was also compatible with iTunes on a computer. Thus another device is a computer, in addition to an iPhone and an iPod."

As for the author of "Lovely Complex" Saki Ukai was born in 1985, but her work partner which is often credited as the author, is Aya Nakahara, however the actual author, as per, the Japanese wiki, is  Aya Nakahara and Kyoko Namiki although it is written that they are co authors and that the manga originally has more than 50 volumes

A: Saki Ukai, an illustrator, best known for the manga series titled "My Teen Romantic Comedy SNAFU" which has a different main character than you mentioned and is actually more popular, is not credited for writing the series you are thinking about. Saki Ukai is however, not the only artist that wrote the manga series you are thinking about. There are many manga series that have a high school setting, but one manga series with a 16 year old high school student named Ichitaka Seto is known as "Lovely Complex". As for the author of "Lovely Complex" Saki Ukai was born in 1985, but her work partner which is often credited as the author, is Aya Nakahara, however the actual author, as per, the Japanese wiki, is  Aya Nakahara and Kyoko Namiki although it is written that they are co authors and that the manga originally has more than 50 volumes with Kyoko Namiki being the author for the additional 3 volumes that were released after Nakahara's passing, although this has yet to be verified on further reliable sources.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: Saki Ukai, an illustrator, best known for the manga series titled "My Teen Romantic Comedy SNAFU" which has a different main character than you mentioned and is actually more popular, is not credited for writing the series you are thinking about. Saki Ukai is however, not the only artist that wrote the manga series you are thinking about. There are many manga series that have a high school setting, but one manga series with a 16 year old high school student named Ichitaka Seto is known as "Lovely Complex". As for the author of "Lovely Complex" Saki Ukai was born in 1985, but her work partner which is often credited as the author, is Aya Nakahara, however the actual author, as per, the Japanese wiki, is  Aya Nakahara and Kyoko Namiki although it is written that they are co authors and that the manga originally has more than 50 volumes with Kyoko Namiki being the author for the additional 3 volumes that were released after Nakahara's passing, although this has yet to be verified on further reliable sources. So, the answer is: 1985.

(In some translations of the book: Kapitsa 1998: 67). It was opened to the public, and was seen as an interesting experiment to reach a wider public by using modern technology.    The discussion forum was initiated in March 1987 by Mikhail Gorbachev. One of the panel's participants was Andrei Sakharov, a Soviet dissident. Another example, is the institution called the  Soviet Discovery's platform  conducted in April 1988 under the writer Yuli Daniel and the editor Andrey Radkov.

A: Justin Timberlake (\"Cry Me a River\" and \"Rock Your Body\") and \"Cry Me a River\" had the song \"Stuck\" by Justin Timberlake stuck behind for eight consecutive weeks ( on the U.S. Billboard Hot 100 chart), but on that song the second alternative is \"Stuck\" is sung

5ae7e1fc55429952e35ea9cc
What color clothing do people of the Netherlands wear during Oranjegekte or to celebrate the national holiday Koningsdag? 
A: []
A: They wear Orange clothing.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: They wear Orange clothing. The name of the Dutch national team, the Dutch national football team, and the Dutch national women's football team is the Oranje.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: They wear Orange clothing. The name of the Dutch national team, the Dutch national football team, and the Dutch national women's football team is the Oranje. Orange is the national color of the Netherlands, it is therefore the color that the Dutch wear on "Orange madness" (Oranjegekte), during the national holiday "King's Day" (Koningsdag), the national football and women's football matches and so on.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: They wear Orange clothing. The name of the Dutch national team, the Dutch national football team, and the Dutch national women's football team is the Oranje. Orange is the national color of the Netherlands, it is therefore the color that the Dutch wear on "Orange madness" (Oranjegekte), during the national holiday "King's Day" (Koningsdag), the national football and women's football matches and so on. So the answer is: Orange.
A: []
Q: What color clothing do people of the Netherlands wear during Oranjegekte or to celebrate the national holiday Koningsdag?
A: "The Dutch national team sports orange jerseys, and during the excitement that surrounds the team (called \"Oranjegekte\") people of the Netherlands wear the color orange to show their enthusiasm. People also wear the color orange to celebrate the national holiday Koningsdag. So the answer is: Orange. (Joro (Talk)) 16:10, 20 February 2021 (UTC)"
A: Orange. (Joro (Talk)) 16:10, 20 February 2021 (UTC)

What was the name of the 1996 loose adaptation of William Shakespeare's "Romeo & Juliet" written by James Gunn?
A: []
A: James Gunn wrote a 1996 loose adaptation of William Shakespeare's "Romeo & Juliet" which was titled "Nobody's Fool".
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: James Gunn wrote a 1996 loose adaptation of William Shakespeare's "Romeo & Juliet" which was titled "Nobody's Fool". 
A: []
Q: What was the name of the 1996 loose adaptation of William Shakespeare's "Romeo & Juliet" written by James Gunn?
A: "James Gunn wrote a 1996 loose adaptation of William Shakespeare's \"Romeo & Juliet\" which was titled \"Nobody's Fool\". However this film is more about a Danny (Christopher Masterson), the youngest son of the family of the Irish-American diamond trader, O'Rourke, and its about his love for the delinquent, loyal and girly Cassidy (Alexis Arquette). This movie has almost no similarity with the story of Romeo and Juliet. The film was written and directed"
A: James Gunn wrote a 1996 loose adaptation of William Shakespeare's \"Romeo & Juliet\" which was titled \"Nobody's Fool\". However this film is more about a Danny (Christopher Masterson), the youngest son of the family of the Irish-American diamond trader, O'Rourke, and its about his love for the delinquent, loyal and girly Cassidy (Alexis Arquette). This movie has almost no similarity with the story of Romeo and Juliet. The film was written and directed

Q: Rostker v. Goldberg held that the practice of what way of filling armed forces vacancies was consitutional?
A: "The Supreme Court in 1981 held that Rostker v. Goldberg that the military's selective-service draft on men only, but not on women was constitutional. Rostker argued that the military could properly not consider women because not enough data was available to properly assess the ability of women to do all the various military jobs.  This 1971 law, the 'Uniformed Services' Imbalance of Needs and Resources Act' that stated that the military needs must be balanced by the use of"
A: The Supreme Court in 1981 held that Rostker v. Goldberg that the military's selective-service draft on men only, but not on women was constitutional. Rostker argued that the military could properly not consider women because not enough data was available to properly assess the ability of women to do all the various military jobs.  This 1971 law, the 'Uniformed Services' Imbalance of Needs and Resources Act' that stated that the military needs must be balanced by the use of

A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: Handi-Snacks are a snack food product line sold by the American multinational confectionery, food, and beverage company Conagra Brands. 
A: []
Q: Handi-Snacks are a snack food product line sold by what American multinational confectionery, food, and beverage company that is based in Illinois?
A: "Handi-Snacks are a snack food product line sold by the American multinational confectionery, food, and beverage company Conagra Brands. They are one of the many brands owned by Conagra Brands that was inherited from the Ralston Purina company in 2001, and are made by Conagra at its Iowa plants."
A: Handi-Snacks are a snack food product line sold by the American multinational confectionery, food, and beverage company Conagra Brands. They are one of the many brands owned by Conagra Brands that was inherited from the Ralston Purina company in 2001, and are made by Conagra at its Iowa plants

Note that a Rabat-Sal\u00e9-K\u00e9nitra administrative region does not exist in Morocco, there is a Rabat-Sal\u00e9-K\u00e9nitra Region. Rabat is the capital of Morocco. Rabat is an autonomous city. A: Unfortunately, I can't verify the ambassador of the Rabat-Sal\u00e9-K\u00e9nitra administrative region to China. A: The base is called Edwards Air Force Base, which is named after Flight Test Center pilot Captain Glen Edwards who crash-landed a North American F-86 Sabre there in 1953, and in the plane test flight that preceded it. (Where The Right Stuff was set for its first landing among other places) Captain Glen Edwards died in the crash. The pilots of flight test was pushing the envelope of jet fighter to make it faster. Flight test pilots have become legendary. In the right stuff film, the most famous test pilot in his day, Chuck Yeager, was the one to break the sound barrier in 1947 by flying an X-1 rocket-powered aircraft at an altitude of about 25,000 ft. over the dry lake at the Muroc Bombing Range in California, on October 14, 1947. The Muroc Bombing Range where the sound barrier was broken in the Bell X-1 experimental rocket powered aircraft was called the Dry Lake Lakebed, and later Edwards Air Force Base was moved there from the dry lake, a lake, which makes it sound less appealing and they called it Edwards AFB and then said, although not literally where the sound barrier was broken, it was called Muroc (see below).
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The base is called Edwards Air Force Base, which is named after Flight Test Center pilot Captain Glen Edwards who crash-landed a North American F-86 Sabre there in 1953, and in the plane test flight that preceded it. (Where The Right Stuff was set for its first landing among other places) Captain Glen Edwards died in the crash. The pilots of flight test was pushing the envelope of jet fighter to make it faster. Flight test pilots have become legendary. In the right stuff film, the most famous test pilot in his day, Chuck Yeager, was the one to break the sound barrier in 1947 by flying an X-1 rocket-powered aircraft at an altitude of about 25,000 ft. over the dry lake at the Muroc Bombing Range in California, on October 14, 1947. The Muroc Bombing Range where the sound barrier was broken in the Bell X-1 experimental rocket powered aircraft was called the Dry Lake Lakebed, and later Edwards Air Force Base was moved there from the dry lake, a lake, which makes it sound less appealing and they called it Edwards AFB and then said, although not literally where the sound barrier was broken, it was called Muroc (see below). Muroc is also called Groom Lake, officially Edwards AFB Dry Lake Bed and is the home of the 6615th Test Squadron (the "Red Hats") and the 416th Flight Test Squadron and the 526th ICBM Systems Test Integration Squadron, which in 2009 was discontinued by DOD.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The base is called Edwards Air Force Base, which is named after Flight Test Center pilot Captain Glen Edwards who crash-landed a North American F-86 Sabre there in 1953, and in the plane test flight that preceded it. (Where The Right Stuff was set for its first landing among other places) Captain Glen Edwards died in the crash. The pilots of flight test was pushing the envelope of jet fighter to make it faster. Flight test pilots have become legendary. In the right stuff film, the most famous test pilot in his day, Chuck Yeager, was the one to break the sound barrier in 1947 by flying an X-1 rocket-powered aircraft at an altitude of about 25,000 ft. over the dry lake at the Muroc Bombing Range in California, on October 14, 1947. The Muroc Bombing Range where the sound barrier was broken in the Bell X-1 experimental rocket powered aircraft was called the Dry Lake Lakebed, and later Edwards Air Force Base was moved there from the dry lake, a lake, which makes it sound less appealing and they called it Edwards AFB and then said, although not literally where the sound barrier was broken, it was called Muroc (see below). Muroc is also called Groom Lake, officially Edwards AFB Dry Lake Bed and is the home of the 6615th Test Squadron (the "Red Hats") and the 416th Flight Test Squadron and the 526th ICBM Systems Test Integration Squadron, which in 2009 was discontinued by DOD. Groom Lake is also where many years later, you will find the longest runway at Edwards Air Force Base, the Groom Lake Lakebed Dry Lake Bed Nellis, operated by the USAF, 16,800 foot runway.
A: []

A: "The base is called Edwards Air Force Base, which is named after Flight Test Center pilot Captain Glen Edwards who crash-landed a North American F-86 Sabre there in 1953, and in the plane test flight that preceded it. So the answer is: Edwards does not have a first name, the text just mentions that the name is Glen.  Just Captain Glen Edwards."
A: Edwards does not have a first name, the text just mentions that the name is Glen.  Just Captain Glen Edwards

What major truck road is located in Backford Cross?
A: []
A: The A41 is a major truck road in North West England.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The A41 is a major truck road in North West England. The village of Backford is situated just to the north of it, roughly 5 miles north of Chester, and it meets the A51 road nearby, however Backford is also about 1.5 miles from the route of the A41 which lies due west of the village.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The A41 is a major truck road in North West England. The village of Backford is situated just to the north of it, roughly 5 miles north of Chester, and it meets the A51 road nearby, however Backford is also about 1.5 miles from the route of the A41 which lies due west of the village. So the answer is: The A41.  
A: []
Q: What major truck road is located in Backford Cross?
A: "The A41 is a major truck road in North West England. It is not clear if it has a section that goes through Backford Cross. In 2006, the road was bypassed in the northwest, a separate project in the southeast was paused in 2013 and resumed and was opened in 2016. It runs from the M54 motorway near Shrewsbury to the M40 motorway near Bicester. It passes through the towns of Newport, Whitchurch,"
A: The A41 is a major truck road in North West England. It is not clear if it has a section that goes through Backford Cross. A: 1st Earl of Mulgrave was the Commander-in-Chief of Ireland from 1801 to 1805.

A: 1st Earl of Mulgrave was the Commander-in-Chief of Ireland from 1801 to 1805. John Malcolm was the Commander-in-Chief of India from 1816 to 1823 and 1835-1841.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: 1st Earl of Mulgrave was the Commander-in-Chief of Ireland from 1801 to 1805. John Malcolm was the Commander-in-Chief of India from 1816 to 1823 and 1835-1841. Robert Clive (1st Baron Clive of Plassey) was the Commander-in-Chief of India and Commander-in-Chief of Ireland at different times but also contributed to the siege of Bharatpur during which the castle was stormed in 1826.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: 1st Earl of Mulgrave was the Commander-in-Chief of Ireland from 1801 to 1805. John Malcolm was the Commander-in-Chief of India from 1816 to 1823 and 1835-1841. Robert Clive (1st Baron Clive of Plassey) was the Commander-in-Chief of India and Commander-in-Chief of Ireland at different times but also contributed to the siege of Bharatpur during which the castle was stormed in 1826. John Malcolm and Robert Clive of Plassey are the two officials.

Robert Clive (1st Baron Clive of Plassey) was the Commander-in-Chief of India and Commander-in-Chief of Ireland at different times but also contributed to the siege of Bharatpur during which the castle was stormed in 1826. John Malcolm and Robert Clive of Plassey are the two officials. So the answer is: John Malcolm and Robert Clive of Plassey.

A: []
Q: The movies Queen of Blood and Battle Beyond the Sun both contained scenes from what 1959 Soviet feature?
A: "According to Wikipedia the movies Queen of Blood and Battle Beyond the Sun both contained scenes from the 1959 Soviet feature, Planetary Principle. Planetary Principle was a Soviet science fiction film directed by Pavel Klushantsev. It was the fourth film in Klushantsev's science fiction trilogy, preceded by The Road to the Stars (1957), New Atlantis (1959) and followed by Planeta Bur (1962). It was directed by Pavel Klushantsev."
A: According to Wikipedia the movies Queen of Blood and Battle Beyond the Sun both contained scenes from the 1959 Soviet feature, Planetary Principle. Planetary Principle was a Soviet science fiction film directed by Pavel Klushantsev. It was the fourth film in Klushantsev's science fiction trilogy, preceded by The Road to the Stars (1957), New Atlantis (1959) and followed by Planeta Bur (1962). It was directed by Pavel Klushantsev

5ac547525542993e66e822a3
In 2017, what was 3/4th of the organization that also has a Center for Veterinary Medicine as well as part of the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, spent on?
A: []
A: In 2017, the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) spent about 3/4th of its budget on paying the salaries of its nearly 17,000 full-time employees.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: In 2017, the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) spent about 3/4th of its budget on paying the salaries of its nearly 17,000 full-time employees. Given that total 2017 FDA budget was $5.7 billion,  3/4th of this total will be 4,200,000,000 or  $4,200,000,000.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: In 2017, the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) spent about 3/4th of its budget on paying the salaries of its nearly 17,000 full-time employees. Given that total 2017 FDA budget was $5.7 billion,  3/4th of this total will be 4,200,000,000 or  $4,200,000,000. Thus the answer is: $4,200,000,000.
A: []
Q: In 2017, what was 3/4th of the organization that also has a Center for Veterinary Medicine as well as part of the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, spent on?
A: "In 2017, the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) spent about 3/4th of its budget on paying the salaries of its nearly 17,000 full-time employees. The FDA is a part of the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS), which also has a Center for Veterinary Medicine and several other offices and institutes."
A: In 2017, the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) spent about 3/4th of its budget on paying the salaries of its nearly 17,000 full-time employees. The FDA is a part of the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS), which also has a Center for Veterinary Medicine and several other offices and institutes

A: Boudica was a Celtic queen, but I couldn't find a reference to a Celtic ruler that was born in AD 43.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: Boudica was a Celtic queen, but I couldn't find a reference to a Celtic ruler that was born in AD 43. However, King Cunobelinus is a historical figure, who ruled southeastern Britain from about AD 9 until AD 40, and his sons, who later ruled his kingdom in various times, were named Caratacus, Togodumnus, and others.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: Boudica was a Celtic queen, but I couldn't find a reference to a Celtic ruler that was born in AD 43. However, King Cunobelinus is a historical figure, who ruled southeastern Britain from about AD 9 until AD 40, and his sons, who later ruled his kingdom in various times, were named Caratacus, Togodumnus, and others. King Cunobelinus is thought to have been of the Catuvellaunian tribe and his kingdom covered parts of modern-day southern England, that was one of the last Celtic kingdoms in Britain, until it was conquered by the Roman Empire.

A: I cannot identify the language. However, according to Wikipedia, the village of Leer in East Frisia, Germany, derives its name from the village of Lerum, which was a small settlement situated on the river Leda opposite the riverbanks where Leer is situated. Lerum is believed to be derived from the Old Frisian word for "wattle" or "reed". Lerum or Leer has been mentioned in the Liber Landwuri, a Carolingian list of names of men in the area, around 770. It was also known as Lirum in the Landfrid manuscript, created in the 8th century.

A: I cannot identify the language. However, according to Wikipedia, the village of Leer in East Frisia, Germany, derives its name from the village of Lerum, which was a small settlement situated on the river Leda opposite the riverbanks where Leer is situated. Lerum is believed to be derived from the Old Frisian word for "wattle" or "reed". Lerum or Leer has been mentioned in the Liber Landwuri, a Carolingian list of names of men in the area, around 770. It was also known as Lirum in the Landfrid manuscript, created in the 8th century.

A: In the 2010 Commonwealth games the Falkland Islands beat England. England is part of the United Kingdom, which has 4 countries and eleven administrative divisions. The other 3 countries of the UK are Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland. The 4 administrative divisions of England are the regions of North East England, North West England, Yorkshire and the Humber, East Midlands, West Midlands, East of England, London, South East England, South West England and the administrative region of the Greater London Authority, and also the official regions are the 48 ceremonial counties of England.

A: []
A: In the 2010 Commonwealth games the Falkland Islands beat England. England is part of the United Kingdom, which has 4 countries and eleven administrative divisions. The other 3 countries of the UK are Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland. The 4 administrative divisions of England are the regions of North East England, North West England, Yorkshire and the Humber, East Midlands, West Midlands, East of England, London, South East England, South West England and the administrative region of the Greater London Authority, and also the official regions are the 48 ceremonial counties of England. The 7 administrative divisions of Scotland are the local government council areas that are in Shetland, Orkney, Na h-Eileanan Siar (Western Isles), Highland, Hebrides, Aberdeen and Dumfries and Galloway, however Scotland also has 32 council areas.

A: In the 2010 Commonwealth games the Falkland Islands beat England. England is part of the United Kingdom, which has 4 countries and eleven administrative divisions. The other 3 countries of the UK are Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland. The 4 administrative divisions of England are the regions of North East England, North West England, Yorkshire and the Humber, East Midlands, West Midlands, East of England, London, South East England, South West England and the administrative region of the Greater London Authority, and also the official regions are the 48 ceremonial counties of England. The 7 administrative divisions of Scotland are the local government council areas that are in Shetland, Orkney, Na h-Eileanan Siar (Western Isles), Highland, Hebrides, Aberdeen and Dumfries and Galloway, however Scotland also has 32 council areas. Wales has three local government regions in North Wales, Mid Wales and South Wales and 22 local government principal areas.

A: []
A: In the 2010 Commonwealth games the Falkland Islands beat England. England is part of the United Kingdom, which has 4 countries and eleven administrative divisions. The other 3 countries of the UK are Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland. The 4 administrative divisions of England are the regions of North East England, North West England, Yorkshire and the Humber, East Midlands, West Midlands, East of England, London, South East England, South West England and the administrative region of the Greater London Authority, and also the official regions are the 48 ceremonial counties of England. The 7 administrative divisions of Scotland are the local government council areas that are in Shetland, Orkney, Na h-Eileanan Siar (Western Isles), Highland, Hebrides, Aberdeen and Dumfries and Galloway, however Scotland also has 32 council areas. Wales has three local government regions in North Wales, Mid Wales and South Wales and 22 local government principal areas. Northern Ireland is divided into six local government districts.

The other 3 countries of the UK are Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland. The 4 administrative divisions of England are the regions of North East England, North West England, Yorkshire and the Humber, East Midlands, West Midlands, East of England, London, South East England, South West England and the administrative region of the Greater London Authority, and also the official regions are the 48 ceremonial counties of England. The 7 administrative divisions of Scotland are the local government council areas that are in Shetland, Orkney, Na h-Eileanan Siar (Western Isles), Highland, Hebrides, Aberdeen and Dumfries and Galloway, however Scotland also has 32 council areas. Wales has three local government regions in North Wales, Mid Wales and South Wales and 22 local government principal areas. Northern Ireland is divided into six local government districts. The other country that has eleven administrative divisions is China.

A: []
A: The Shiners' War took place in the 17th century in the Eastern Townships of what is now the province of Quebec. According to various sources, it involved the conflict between the Acadian ethnic group and Irish Catholic immigrants. So the answer is: Acadian.
A: []
Q: What is the name of the ethnic group who settled in Canada in the 17th century that participated in a conflict with Irish Catholic immigrants in the Shiners' War?
A: "The Shiners' War took place in the 17th century in the Eastern Townships of what is now the province of Quebec. The participants were from the ethnic group Acadian, who settled in Canada in the 17th century, and the Irish Catholic immigrants."

A: The Öresund Bridge is 16,000 meters long.

A: []
A: The Öresund Bridge is 16,000 meters long. The bridge connects Copenhagen, Denmark and Malmö, Sweden. It was opened on July 1, 2000.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The Öresund Bridge is 16,000 meters long. The bridge connects Copenhagen, Denmark and Malmö, Sweden. It was opened on July 1, 2000. The suspension bridge is 7,845 meters long, a 4-kilometer artificial island named Peberholm in the middle and a 4,080-meter long railway and motorway bridge called the Drogden Tunnel (also known as the Drogden Bridge).
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The Öresund Bridge is 16,000 meters long. The bridge connects Copenhagen, Denmark and Malmö, Sweden. It was opened on July 1, 2000. The suspension bridge is 7,845 meters long, a 4-kilometer artificial island named Peberholm in the middle and a 4,080-meter long railway and motorway bridge called the Drogden Tunnel (also known as the Drogden Bridge). The tunnel is 4,060 meters long.

A: []
A: The Öresund Bridge is 16,000 meters long. The bridge connects Copenhagen, Denmark and Malmö, Sweden. It was opened on July 1, 2000. The suspension bridge is 7,845 meters long, a 4-kilometer artificial island named Peberholm in the middle and a 4,080-meter long railway and motorway bridge called the Drogden Tunnel (also known as the Drogden Bridge). The tunnel is 4,060 meters long. The main bridge has a height of 57 meters above the water level.

A: The Öresund Bridge is 16,000 meters long. The bridge connects Copenhagen, Denmark and Malmö, Sweden. It was opened on July 1, 2000. A: The Öresund Bridge is 16,000 meters long. The bridge connects Copenhagen, Denmark and Malmö, Sweden. It was opened on July 1, 2000. The suspension bridge is 7,845 meters long, a 4-kilometer artificial island named Peberholm in the middle and a 4,080-meter long railway and motorway bridge called the Drogden Tunnel (also known as the Drogden Bridge). The tunnel is 4,060 meters long. The main bridge has a height of 57 meters above the water level. The peak height above sea level is 65 meters. The height of the towers is 204 meters.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The Öresund Bridge is 16,000 meters long. The bridge connects Copenhagen, Denmark and Malmö, Sweden. It was opened on July 1, 2000. The suspension bridge is 7,845 meters long, a 4-kilometer artificial island named Peberholm in the middle and a 4,080-meter long railway and motorway bridge called the Drogden Tunnel (also known as the Drogden Bridge). The tunnel is 4,060 meters long. The main bridge has a height of 57 meters above the water level. The peak height above sea level is 65 meters. The height of the towers is 204 meters. The daily toll for driving is around 365 DKK (approximately 50 EUR).
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The Öresund Bridge is 16,000 meters long. The bridge connects Copenhagen, Denmark and Malmö, Sweden. It was opened on July 1, 2000. The suspension bridge is 7,845 meters long, a 4-kilometer artificial island named Peberholm in the middle and a 4,080-meter long railway and motorway bridge called the Drogden Tunnel (also known as the Drogden Bridge). The tunnel is 4,060 meters long. The main bridge has a height of 57 meters above the water level. The peak height above sea level is 65 meters. The height of the towers is 204 meters. The daily toll for driving is around 365 DKK (approximately 50 EUR). The name is from the Öresund Strait that separates the two countries, which is 6.8 miles (11 kilometers) wide at the narrowest point.

5ab8688b55429934fafe6daa

A: []
A: John Lee invented a new type of knitting needle holder. He was not a hairdresser but an inventor. However, the material is not named by his granddaughter, but by him. The material was named 'Lee's Plasticine' or 'Lee's Plastic'.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: John Lee invented a new type of knitting needle holder. He was not a hairdresser but an inventor. However, the material is not named by his granddaughter, but by him. The material was named 'Lee's Plasticine' or 'Lee's Plastic'. Lee invented this modelling material which his granddaughter called Plasticine.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: John Lee invented a new type of knitting needle holder. He was not a hairdresser but an inventor. However, the material is not named by his granddaughter, but by him. The material was named 'Lee's Plasticine' or 'Lee's Plastic'. Lee invented this modelling material which his granddaughter called Plasticine. So the answer is: Plasticine.
A: []
Q: What material did a hairdresser from Yorkshire, England invent that was named by his granddaughter?
A: "John Lee invented a new type of knitting needle holder. He invented a machine to wind (or card) wool, which made spinning wool into yarn easier for farmers. He also invented the curved (or \"dingley\") electric hair curlers and invented or promoted a corset-style belt for men. His granddaughter, Ann, said he invented \"curled hair\", which she named \"curly hair.\" He invented an early version of a curling iron. John Lee also invented a butyl"
A: John Lee invented a new type of knitting needle holder. He invented a machine to wind (or card) wool, which made spinning wool into yarn easier for farmers. He also invented the curved (or \"dingley\") electric hair curlers and invented or promoted a corset-style belt for men. His granddaughter, Ann, said he invented \"curled hair\", which she named \"curly hair.\" He invented an early version of a curling iron. John Lee also invented a butyl

5a7a3a945542996a35c17147
One Raffles Place is one of the tallest skyscrapers in the city of Singapore and tallest in the wolrd outside North America until it was succeeded by a Building in city?
A: []
A: The 73-storey, 280-meter One Raffles Place in Singapore was the world's tallest completed building outside North America, but was surpassed by a skyscraper in another city in 1992.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The 73-storey, 280-meter One Raffles Place in Singapore was the world's tallest completed building outside North America, but was surpassed by a skyscraper in another city in 1992. This building was the 85-storey (310.3 meters)
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The 73-storey, 280-meter One Raffles Place in Singapore was the world's tallest completed building outside North America, but was surpassed by a skyscraper in another city in 1992. This building was the 85-storey (310.3 meters) Petronas Twin Towers in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia's capital city.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The 73-storey, 280-meter One Raffles Place in Singapore was the world's tallest completed building outside North America, but was surpassed by a skyscraper in another city in 1992. This building was the 85-storey (310.3 meters) Petronas Twin Towers in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia's capital city. One Raffles Place was the tallest building in Singapore and the tallest building in the city of Singapore until 1992.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The 73-storey, 280-meter One Raffles Place in Singapore was the world's tallest completed building outside North America, but was surpassed by a skyscraper in another city in 1992. This building was the 85-storey (310.3 meters) Petronas Twin Towers in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia's capital city. One Raffles Place was the tallest building in Singapore and the tallest building in the city of Singapore until 1992. So the answer is: Kuala Lumpur.
A: []
Q: One Raffles Place is one of the tallest skyscrapers in the city of Singapore and tallest in the wolrd outside North America until it was succeeded by a Building in city?
A: "The 73-storey, 280-meter One Raffles Place in Singapore was the world's tallest completed building outside North America, but was surpassed by a skyscraper in another city in 1992. The Singapore's 73-storey, 280-meter One Raffles Place was in 1992, succeeded by a building in the city of Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia. So the answer is: Kuala Lumpur."
A: Kuala Lumpur

Q: The Kansas City Wizards have had three home venues in Kansas City in their time.  Which was their first?
A: "The team was founded in 1996, and their first home was at Kansas University's Rock Chalk Park. This must be their first home. The Rock Chalk Park is also known as the historic Municipal Stadium and the historic Community America Ballpark. It was not known as Rock Chalk Park prior to 1996. However the now defunct Professional Soccer Team, the Kansas City F.C. was established 1978 and would not begin competing in the 1996 NASL expansion season"
A: The team was founded in 1996, and their first home was at Kansas University's Rock Chalk Park. This must be their first home. The Rock Chalk Park is also known as the historic Municipal Stadium and the historic Community America Ballpark. It was not known as Rock Chalk Park prior to 1996. However the now defunct Professional Soccer Team, the Kansas City F.C. was established 1978 and would not begin competing in the 1996 NASL expansion season

A: The airport is Boston Logan International Airport (BOS). The airport serviced by American Airlines Shuttle is Boston Logan International Airport (BOS). The airport is the largest airport in the New England region and the 17th busiest in the United States. So the answer is: Boston Logan International Airport (BOS).
A: []
Q: What airport serviced by American Airlines Shuttle, is also the largest airport in the New England region and 17th busiest in the U.S?
A: "The airport is Boston Logan International Airport (BOS). Boston Logan is the largest airport in the New England region. BOS is the IATA airport code for Boston Logan. American Airlines Shuttle operates services from BOS airport. So the answer is: Boston Logan International Airport (BOS)."
A: Boston Logan International Airport (BOS)

5a85cead5542991dd0999ea9
What arcade game is named after the only player in major league history to have a 0.300 batting average for 7 consecutive seasons?
A: []
A: The arcade game is called Cy Young for the home run ace, not for the batting leader, but rather it is called Roger Maris for Roger Maris and Cy Young's record was broken in the 1961 season by Roger Maris.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The arcade game is called Cy Young for the home run ace, not for the batting leader, but rather it is called Roger Maris for Roger Maris and Cy Young's record was broken in the 1961 season by Roger Maris. The answer is: Not given in choices, but the correct answer is Roger Maris.
A: []
Q: What arcade game is named after the only player in major league history to have a 0.300 batting average for 7 consecutive seasons?
A: "The arcade game is called Cy Young for the home run ace, not for the batting leader, but rather it is called Roger Maris for Roger Maris and Cy Young's record was broken in the 1961 season by Roger Maris. The batting leader of 7 consecutive seasons is however Ty Cobb, and the answer is Ty Cobb Baseball. The arcade game is also known as Ty Cobb Baseball, which has variously been called the first baseball video game and the first video game that appealed to"
A: Ty Cobb Baseball. The arcade game is also known as Ty Cobb Baseball, which has variously been called the first baseball video game and the first video game that appealed to

Q: Which professional baseball player and National 1993 Baseball Hall of Fame inductee is featured in the Greatest Sports Legends anthology?
A: "The book \"The Greatest Sports Legends\" features several professional baseball players who were inducted into the Hall of Fame including Eddie Collins and Ernie Banks but notably, this series does not have Eddie Collins featured in any anthology however, in 1999, a comprehensive revision was published containing 101 Baseball Legends including Ken Griffey Jr, Willie Mays but not notable inductee, that author \u2013 Fred Ferretti  chose instead to include Hall of Fame player Harry Hooper but notably, Harry Ho"
A: The book \"The Greatest Sports Legends\" features several professional baseball players who were inducted into the Hall of Fame including Eddie Collins and Ernie Banks but notably, this series does not have Eddie Collins featured in any anthology however, in 1999, a comprehensive revision was published containing 101 Baseball Legends including Ken Griffey Jr, Willie Mays but not notable inductee, that author \u2013 Fred Ferretti  chose instead to include Hall of Fame player Harry Hooper but notably, Harry Ho

A: Salisbury Woodland Gardens links a zoo with a park designed and built under the watchful eye of John Lee. The Salisbury Woodland Gardens is located in De Soto, Missouri. It includes a botanical garden, an aviary, and a small animal zoo and is home to 110 different species of plants.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: Salisbury Woodland Gardens links a zoo with a park designed and built under the watchful eye of John Lee. The Salisbury Woodland Gardens is located in De Soto, Missouri. It includes a botanical garden, an aviary, and a small animal zoo and is home to 110 different species of plants. The garden is located on the premises of the University of Missouri–Kansas City at its University Field Station.

A: Salisbury Woodland Gardens links a zoo with a park designed and built under the watchful eye of John Lee. The Salisbury Woodland Gardens is located in De Soto, Missouri. It includes a botanical garden, an aviary, and a small animal zoo and is home to 110 different species of plants. The garden is located on the premises of the University of Missouri–Kansas City at its University Field Station. There are areas for walking, picnicking, or attending nature programs, and the garden is also the site of the Kwan:zo theatrical garden.

A: Salisbury Woodland Gardens links a zoo with a park designed and built under the watchful eye of John Lee. The Salisbury Woodland Gardens is located in De Soto, Missouri. It includes a botanical garden, an aviary, and a small animal zoo and is home to 110 different species of plants. The garden is located on the premises of the University of Missouri–Kansas City at its University Field Station. There are areas for walking, picnicking, or attending nature programs, and the garden is also the site of the Kwan:zo theatrical garden. It has trails for hiking, walking, and jogging. The gardens are managed by the Friends of the University of Missouri–Kansas City, a non-profit organization that accepts donations. It is the only American botanical garden dedicated to tropical plants that was designed and built by the British landscape designer and architect Reginald MacColl.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: Salisbury Woodland Gardens links a zoo with a park designed and built under the watchful eye of John Lee. The Salisbury Woodland Gardens is located in De Soto, Missouri. It includes a botanical garden, an aviary, and a small animal zoo and is home to 110 different species of plants. The garden is located on the premises of the University of Missouri–Kansas City at its University Field Station. There are areas for walking, picnicking, or attending nature programs, and the garden is also the site of the Kwan:zo theatrical garden. It has trails for hiking, walking, and jogging. The gardens are managed by the Friends of the University of Missouri–Kansas City, a non-profit organization that accepts donations. It is the only American botanical garden dedicated to tropical plants that was designed and built by the British landscape designer and architect Reginald MacColl. The garden was originally a 196-acre (79 ha) property where Missouri landowners William and Mabel Winter built the two and one-half story brick dwelling of what they had originally referred to as  “winter’s wooden wings” or  Winter Homestead in 1926.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: Salisbury Woodland Gardens links a zoo with a park designed and built under the watchful eye of John Lee. The Salisbury Woodland Gardens is located in De Soto, Missouri. It includes a botanical garden, an aviary, and a small animal zoo and is home to 110 different species of plants. The garden is located on the premises of the University of Missouri–Kansas City at its University Field Station. There are areas for walking, picnicking, or attending nature programs, and the garden is also the site of the Kwan:zo theatrical garden. It has trails for hiking, walking, and jogging. The gardens are managed by the Friends of the University of Missouri–Kansas City, a non-profit organization that accepts donations. It is the only American botanical garden dedicated to tropical plants that was designed and built by the British landscape designer and architect Reginald MacColl. The garden was originally a 196-acre (79 ha) property where Missouri landowners William and Mabel Winter built the two and one-half story brick dwelling of what they had originally referred to as  “winter’s wooden wings” or  Winter Homestead in 1926. In 1931 William winter and his brother worked with University of Missouri–Kansas City administrators to expand the property, following plans designed by a garden specialist named William lEast, creating the zoo.

The House of Windsor is a European royal house of German origin, which has been"

A: "In Shakespeare's Romeo and Juliet, Benvolio is a close friend of Romeo. In the same play, it is not Benvolio that slays. Instead, it is Tybalt that fights and then gets slain by Romeo. Thus, it is Mercutio that slays Tybalt as a part of a duel. So the answer is: Mercutio. There is a protagonist that does have secret love but slays Benvolio with his off-hand, that character is"

A: []
A: Creatine is commonly used in bodybuilding, also known as AAS (anabolic androgenic steroids). So the answer is: Creatine.
A: []
Q: What, known as AAS, is commonly used in bodybuilding?
A: "Creatine is commonly used in bodybuilding, also known as AAS (anabolic androgenic steroids). It can improve physical performance and increase muscle mass, strength, and power during exercise. AAS is used to describe synthetic testosterone, including methandrostenolone. Creatine, however, does not fit this description."
A: Creatine is commonly used in bodybuilding, also known as AAS (anabolic androgenic steroids). It can improve physical performance and increase muscle mass, strength, and power during exercise. AAS is used to describe synthetic testosterone, including methandrostenolone. Creatine, however, does not fit this description

A: Glenn Branca. Note: Other notable works by Branca include Symphony No. 13 and Sunpower. However, these pieces were not created for, and first performed at, Symphony Center in Chicago in 2012. Night Ferry is the correct answer for this question. And Symphony No. 13 is Glenn Branca's most well known work. It is a 13-instrument guitar symphony composed in 1984. It consists of a single note, A (440 hertz), sustained for 20 minutes. The work explores the variable timbres of a group of guitarists playing individual six-string instruments.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: Glenn Branca. Note: Other notable works by Branca include Symphony No. 13 and Sunpower. However, these pieces were not created for, and first performed at, Symphony Center in Chicago in 2012. Night Ferry is the correct answer for this question. And Symphony No. 13 is Glenn Branca's most well known work. It is a 13-instrument guitar symphony composed in 1984. It consists of a single note, A (440 hertz), sustained for 20 minutes. The work explores the variable timbres of a group of guitarists playing individual six-string instruments. The work has been performed by a variety of ensembles and has been described as "an astonishing sonic experience".
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: Glenn Branca. Note: Other notable works by Branca include Symphony No. 13 and Sunpower. However, these pieces were not created for, and first performed at, Symphony Center in Chicago in 2012. Night Ferry is the correct answer for this question. And Symphony No. 13 is Glenn Branca's most well known work. It is a 13-instrument guitar symphony composed in 1984. It consists of a single note, A (440 hertz), sustained for 20 minutes. The work explores the variable timbres of a group of guitarists playing individual six-string instruments. The work has been performed by a variety of ensembles and has been described as "an astonishing sonic experience". The work was recorded in 1987 and released on the CD "Symphony N° 13" on the Ork Records label.

(One per aircraft carrier) The VMF-213 was a Marine Corps fighter squadron that flew the F4U Corsair during World War II. A: During World War II the VMF-213 was assigned to the USS Saratoga (CV-3) and the USS Enterprise (CV-6). Therefore, there are two correct answers. (One per aircraft carrier) The VMF-213 was a Marine Corps fighter squadron that flew the F4U Corsair during World War II. When the squadron was activated in 1942, it was the first Marine squadron to be equipped with the F4U. It was reassigned a number of times during the war. During the Battle of Guadalcanal, the squadron used Henderson Field. During the Battle of Okinawa, the squadron used Yontan Airfield. The squadron also operated from Kwajalein, Eniwetok, and Iwo Jima.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: During World War II the VMF-213 was assigned to the USS Saratoga (CV-3) and the USS Enterprise (CV-6). Therefore, there are two correct answers. (One per aircraft carrier) The VMF-213 was a Marine Corps fighter squadron that flew the F4U Corsair during World War II. When the squadron was activated in 1942, it was the first Marine squadron to be equipped with the F4U. It was reassigned a number of times during the war. During the Battle of Guadalcanal, the squadron used Henderson Field. During the Battle of Okinawa, the squadron used Yontan Airfield. The squadron also operated from Kwajalein, Eniwetok, and Iwo Jima. As the island-hopping campaign continued, the squadron also operated from Saipan and Tinian.
A: []
Q: To which aircraft carrier was the VMF-213 Marine Fighting Squadron assigned to durning World War II?
A: "During World War II the VMF-213 was assigned to the USS Saratoga (CV-3) and the USS Enterprise (CV-6). However, at some point they were moved to the USS Bunker Hill (CV-17). The answer to your question is that they were assigned to three different aircraft carriers: USS Saratoga, USS Enterprise, and USS Bunker Hill."
A: During World War II the VMF-213 was assigned to the USS Saratoga (CV-3) and the USS Enterprise (CV-6). However, at some point they were moved to the USS Bunker Hill (CV-17). The answer to your question is that they were assigned to three different aircraft carriers: USS Saratoga, USS Enterprise, and USS Bunker Hill

Q: What movie did Pitof direct which had an action-adventure tie-in video game based off of it in 2004?
A: "Pitof directed the movie \"Catwoman\". The action-adventure tie-in video game \"Catwoman\" was released in 2004. In the game, the player controlled the character of Patience Phillips (played by Halle Berry in the movie). The game had a variety of styles and options for each mission, which could influence gameplay and the player's character in the game."
A: Pitof directed the movie \"Catwoman\". The action-adventure tie-in video game \"Catwoman\" was released in 2004. In the game, the player controlled the character of Patience Phillips (played by Halle Berry in the movie). The game had a variety of styles and options for each mission, which could influence gameplay and the player's character in the game

A: The song album for the 2007 Broadway musical, Grease was recorded by the cast. In an interview from 2007 of Mark Isaacson, Isaacson, who is a literary publicist and a former co-director of the Drama Book Shop in New York City mentions a couple of notable actresses that have received a Drama Desk award, for instance Emily Skinner, Christine Ebersole and Sierra Boggess but also mentions: Katie Finneran, "this somebody actually won two  Tony Awards and Drama Desk Awards". The people Katie Finneran, Tony Award winner for the musicals "Promises, Promises" in 2010 and "Barefoot in the Park" in 2012 also took part in the cast for Grease. But the people that can't be excluded are: Donna Murphy for passing the Actor's Studio program, though the number of Drama Desk awards of this Tony Award-winner, who starred in "Wonderland", a musical of Alice in Wonderland is, indeed, one and Chiara Schipano for playing the female lead for short and faded, that is an off-Broadway production. Somebody, however, seems to have some other side project somewhere else and one of the persons of this side project has a notable experience with Disney.

Q: What is the name of the actress who has received a Drama Desk award and was part of the cast album for the 2007 Broadway production of the hit musical, "Grease"?
A: "The song album for the 2007 Broadway musical, Grease was recorded by the cast. The Drama Desk award for Outstanding Ensemble in a Musical was awarded to the cast of Grease, but no specific cast member is mentioned in the Wikipedia article for that category. However, Julia Murney and Laura Osnes, both of whom are listed as part of the cast in the Wikipedia article for the 2007 Broadway production of Grease, are both listed in the Wikipedia article for the cast album"
A: The song album for the 2007 Broadway musical, Grease was recorded by the cast. The Drama Desk award for Outstanding Ensemble in a Musical was awarded to the cast of Grease, but no specific cast member is mentioned in the Wikipedia article for that category. However, Julia Murney and Laura Osnes, both of whom are listed as part of the cast in the Wikipedia article for the 2007 Broadway production of Grease, are both listed in the Wikipedia article for the cast album

A: "The band formed in the Alberta city is Edmonton. I believe it is the band named in the question: Hot Hot Heat. However, there seems to be a better choice for this question, and that band is: I Mother Earth. I'd Come for You is indeed a single by I Mother Earth, a Canadian rock band formed in 1990 in the Canadian province of Alberta. The band's name is derived from the John Lennon song \"Mother\" with the first word changed to \"I\""

Q: Where did Ian Harland study prior to studying at the oldest college at the University of Cambridge?
A: "Ian Harland studied at Upper Canada College in Toronto. Before this, Ian Harland had attended Ashbury College in Ottawa. Prior to that, he studied at Mount Loyden Public School in Kingston. Before this, he had been educated at Aylmer Public School. So, before studying at the oldest college of the University of Cambridge, he studied at Aylmer Public School."
A: Ian Harland studied at Upper Canada College in Toronto. Before this, Ian Harland had attended Ashbury College in Ottawa. Prior to that, he studied at Mount Loyden Public School in Kingston. Before this, he had been educated at Aylmer Public School. So, before studying at the oldest college of the University of Cambridge, he studied at Aylmer Public School

Q: What record did the Super Bowl XXXIV champions finish the regular-season with in 1999?
A: "The St. Louis Rams, who are the champions of Super Bowl XXXIV finished the regular-season in 1999 with an NFL record 14-2. The record was also the best regular season record in the NFL since the 1982 and 1984 San Francisco 49ers who both had a record of 14-2 in the strike-shortened seasons. The Rams completed the regular season with a 14-2 record to tie the league record for the most wins since the"
A: The St. Louis Rams, who are the champions of Super Bowl XXXIV finished the regular-season in 1999 with an NFL record 14-2. The record was also the best regular season record in the NFL since the 1982 and 1984 San Francisco 49ers who both had a record of 14-2 in the strike-shortened seasons. The Rams completed the regular season with a 14-2 record to tie the league record for the most wins since the

A: The town at the intersection of Interstate Highways 77 and the longest Interstate highway within the borders of Virginia is Wytheville.   The population of Wytheville is 8,299. Interstate 77 is a north–south Interstate Highway in the eastern United States. It runs from Columbia, South Carolina, to Portsmouth, Ohio, for a total of 682 miles (1,100 km). Interstate 64 is a north–south Interstate Highway that runs from the Virginia/North Carolina border in Chesapeake, Virginia to St. Louis, Missouri for a total distance of 838 miles (1,346 km) making it the longest Interstate Highway within the borders of Virginia. Interstate 64 and Interstate 77 intersect at an interchange in Wytheville. The intersection is located near the Wytheville–Patrick County line and about 35 miles (56 km) north of the North Carolina border. Wytheville is a town in Wythe County, Virginia. Q: What is the population of the town at the intersection of Interstate Highways 77 and  the longest Interstate highway within the borders of Virginia?
A: "The town at the intersection of Interstate Highways 77 and the longest Interstate highway within the borders of Virginia is Wytheville.  The population of Wytheville is 7,974.  Interstate Highway 77  ends at Interstate Highway 81 in the city of Wytheville and Interstate Highway 81 runs from Tennessee to Delaware, making it the longest Interstate highway within the borders of Virginia. So the answer is: 7,974."
A: 7,974

Q: What was the position of Jean Baptiste Androuet du Cerceau under the man who was King of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth from 1573 to 1575?
A: "The answer is not found on Wikipedia but this part of Wikipedia can give you some information:  Stephen B\u00e1thory of Poland. Stephen B\u00e1thory of Poland was the King of Poland and Grand Duke of Lithuania from 1576 until 1586. He was also Prince of Transylvania. Before becoming king, Stephen was in a position to influence the king of Poland during the period that he refers to, which was 1573 to 1575, for he was Voivode"
A: not found on Wikipedia but this part of Wikipedia can give you some information:  Stephen B\u00e1thory of Poland. Stephen B\u00e1thory of Poland was the King of Poland and Grand Duke of Lithuania from 1576 until 1586. He was also Prince of Transylvania. Before becoming king, Stephen was in a position to influence the king of Poland during the period that he refers to, which was 1573 to 1575, for he was Voivode

A: []
A: The namesake of the ship launched as the München in 1930, Charles Lee, fought in the army of the Kingdom of France during the American Revolutionary War. Charles Lee was the commander-in-chief of the Continental Army before General George Washington.    Charles Lee fled his command and joined the British forces, revealing important information to the British about the American forces. He then was imprisoned and not released until he agreed not to fight against the British. Lee wrote a successful memoir called "Memoirs of the War in the Southern Department of the United States" which was translated from the French, in which he portrayed the British positively, and portrayed George Washington in a bad light, claiming he had schemed against him.

A: []
A: The namesake of the ship launched as the München in 1930, Charles Lee, fought in the army of the Kingdom of France during the American Revolutionary War. Charles Lee was the commander-in-chief of the Continental Army before General George Washington.    Charles Lee fled his command and joined the British forces, revealing important information to the British about the American forces. He then was imprisoned and not released until he agreed not to fight against the British. Lee wrote a successful memoir called "Memoirs of the War in the Southern Department of the United States" which was translated from the French, in which he portrayed the British positively, and portrayed George Washington in a bad light, claiming he had schemed against him. Lee was not trusted by the Americans after this, and although he did visit England, he returned to America and married and had two children there.

Lee was not trusted by the Americans after this, and although he did visit England, he returned to America and married and had two children there. He died in 1818.

Lee wrote a successful memoir called "Memoirs of the War in the Southern Department of the United States" which was translated from the French, in which he portrayed the British positively, and portrayed George Washington in a bad light, claiming he had schemed against him. Lee was not trusted by the Americans after this, and although he did visit England, he returned to America and married and had two children there. He died in 1818. 5a8ee4315542990e94052ba7
What officially ended the first phase of the military conflict between the British Raj and the Emirate of Afghanistan from 1878 to 1880?
A: []
A: The Battle of Maiward (also known as the Battle of Maiwand) was fought on July 27, 1880, and it officially ended the first phase of the military conflict between the British Raj and the Emirate of Afghanistan from 1878 to 1880.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The Battle of Maiward (also known as the Battle of Maiwand) was fought on July 27, 1880, and it officially ended the first phase of the military conflict between the British Raj and the Emirate of Afghanistan from 1878 to 1880. The war was part of a series of conflicts and skirmishes in the region known as the Second Anglo-Afghan War (1878-1880), and it was followed by a lengthy occupation of Afghanistan by British forces.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The Battle of Maiward (also known as the Battle of Maiwand) was fought on July 27, 1880, and it officially ended the first phase of the military conflict between the British Raj and the Emirate of Afghanistan from 1878 to 1880. The war was part of a series of conflicts and skirmishes in the region known as the Second Anglo-Afghan War (1878-1880), and it was followed by a lengthy occupation of Afghanistan by British forces. British casualties at the Battle of Maiwand were around 400 men; however, it led to a reinforcement of 10,000 British troops and helped end the second Anglo-Afghan war in 1880.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The Battle of Maiward (also known as the Battle of Maiwand) was fought on July 27, 1880, and it officially ended the first phase of the military conflict between the British Raj and the Emirate of Afghanistan from 1878 to 1880. The war was part of a series of conflicts and skirmishes in the region known as the Second Anglo-Afghan War (1878-1880), and it was followed by a lengthy occupation of Afghanistan by British forces. British casualties at the Battle of Maiwand were around 400 men; however, it led to a reinforcement of 10,000 British troops and helped end the second Anglo-Afghan war in 1880. Other sources give slightly varying figures, which are claimed to be about 300 killed, not 400.

A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The Battle of Maiward (also known as the Battle of Maiwand) was fought on July 27, 1880, and it officially ended the first phase of the military conflict between the British Raj and the Emirate of Afghanistan from 1878 to 1880. The war was part of a series of conflicts and skirmishes in the region known as the Second Anglo-Afghan War (1878-1880), and it was followed by a lengthy occupation of Afghanistan by British forces. British casualties at the Battle of Maiwand were around 400 men; however, it led to a reinforcement of 10,000 British troops and helped end the second Anglo-Afghan war in 1880. Other sources give slightly varying figures, which are claimed to be about 300 killed, not 400. Nonetheless, there is no contention over the outcome of the event: the date of the Second Anglo-Afghan war was 1878 to 1880.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The Battle of Maiward (also known as the Battle of Maiwand) was fought on July 27, 1880, and it officially ended the first phase of the military conflict between the British Raj and the Emirate of Afghanistan from 1878 to 1880. The war was part of a series of conflicts and skirmishes in the region known as the Second Anglo-Afghan War (1878-1880), and it was followed by a lengthy occupation of Afghanistan by British forces. British casualties at the Battle of Maiwand were around 400 men; however, it led to a reinforcement of 10,000 British troops and helped end the second Anglo-Afghan war in 1880. Other sources give slightly varying figures, which are claimed to be about 300 killed, not 400. Nonetheless, there is no contention over the outcome of the event: the date of the Second Anglo-Afghan war was 1878 to 1880. So the answer is: The Battle of Maiward (also known as the Battle of Maiwand).
A: []
Q: What officially ended the first phase of the military conflict between the British Raj and the Emirate of Afghanistan from 1878 to 1880?
A: "The Battle of Maiward (also known as the Battle of Maiwand) was fought on July 27, 1880, and it officially ended the first phase of the military conflict between the British Raj and the Emirate of Afghanistan from 1878 to 1880. However, the battle also marked the beginning of a second phase of the conflict which ended with the signing of the Treaty of Gandamak on May 26, 1880."
A: The Battle of Maiward (also known as the Battle of Maiwand) was fought on July 27, 1880, and it officially ended the first phase of the military conflict between the British Raj and the Emirate of Afghanistan from 1878 to 1880. However, the battle also marked the beginning of a second phase of the conflict which ended with the signing of the Treaty of Gandamak on May 26, 1880

What is the uppermost age range for the sort of fiction written by Alexander Gordon Smith?
A: []
A: The sort of fiction that the author Alexander Gordon Smith is best known for writing, are supernatural horror novels and short stories, written for teenagers and young adults.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The sort of fiction that the author Alexander Gordon Smith is best known for writing, are supernatural horror novels and short stories, written for teenagers and young adults. His series includes "Eli's Destiny", "The Devil's Door", "The Grim", "The Outcast", and "Hellraiser", which are the prequel novels to Clive Barker's "Hellraiser" series.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The sort of fiction that the author Alexander Gordon Smith is best known for writing, are supernatural horror novels and short stories, written for teenagers and young adults. His series includes "Eli's Destiny", "The Devil's Door", "The Grim", "The Outcast", and "Hellraiser", which are the prequel novels to Clive Barker's "Hellraiser" series. His novels are typically in the upper range of YA literature (Young Adult), but are sometimes classified as crossover literature which can include children.
A: Exit? No.
A: []

A: []
A: Yes, Skin Yard was an American alternative rock band formed in Seattle, Washington, in 1986, but based in Olympia, Washington. Ostava is an indie rock band from Chico, California. Their debut album, "Through the Heart", was released on April 10, 2007 through Stickfigure Records. George Hinchliffe, member of the Bonzo Dog Doo-Dah Band and the Bonzo Dog Doodah Band, also joined Ostava.   The other members are David Kennedy, Raymond Stevens and Michael Thompson. They have been described as post-punk, and the band has been compared to  Wire, Can, and The Velvet Underground,   as well as early Jane's Addiction.    Their music, like that of Skin Yard, is described as "more instrumental than average,".    . Skin Yard was an American alternative rock band formed in Seattle, Washington, in 1986, but based in Olympia, Washington,  and the band's lead vocalist Andy Kessler has stated that in 1988, the band "decided to play as many shows as possible". Their live performances were  often performed at bars, and restaurants with which the band shared a practice space, as well as  VFW halls.

A: []
A: The answer is no.

Q: Are  Chrysalis and Look both women's magazines?
A: "The answer is no.  Chrysalis  is the United States's oldest continuously publishing women's magazine. It was launched in 1977.  Look, on the other hand, is a women's fashion magazine that was in print from 1922 until 2001. It was part of Cond\u00e9 Nast Publications. So, Chrysalis and Look are both women's magazines, but one is still in print and the other is not. Although they are both women's"
A: no.  Chrysalis  is the United States's oldest continuously publishing women's magazine. It was launched in 1977.  Look, on the other hand, is a women's fashion magazine that was in print from 1922 until 2001. It was part of Cond\u00e9 Nast Publications. So, Chrysalis and Look are both women's magazines, but one is still in print and the other is not. Although they are both women's

Q: Which author has won more Pulitzer Prizes, Thomas Friedman or Henri Bergson?
A: "Henri Bergson won the Pulitzer Prize in 1959, and Thomas Friedman won the Pulitzer Prize in 1983. Both authors won the Pulitzer Prize for Public Service. However, Thomas Friedman also won the Pulitzer Prize for Commentary, whereas Henri Bergson won the Pulitzer Prize in 1929 for Journalism. It was this award of 1929 that later became the the Pulitzer Prize for Biography or Autobiography. Also, the German-born French philosopher, Henri Bergson, did not win the Pulitzer"
A: Henri Bergson won the Pulitzer Prize in 1959, and Thomas Friedman won the Pulitzer Prize in 1983. Both authors won the Pulitzer Prize for Public Service. Q: In what city can a person find both the Nusretiye Clock Tower and the Eski Imaret Mosque be found?
A: "Nusretiye Clock Tower and Eski Imaret Mosque are located in Istanbul, Turkey, but more specifically in Fatih district in the city center. The clock tower is located on the second hill of the Old City, overlooking the Golden Horn. Both buildings are historic landmarks. Eski Imaret Mosque is the oldest mosque in the historic Sultanahmet district of Istanbul, and was the first imperial mosque in Ottoman times. The mosque has undergone several renovations since its construction in 1453. It"
A: Nusretiye Clock Tower and Eski Imaret Mosque are located in Istanbul, Turkey, but more specifically in Fatih district in the city center. The clock tower is located on the second hill of the Old City, overlooking the Golden Horn. Both buildings are historic landmarks. Eski Imaret Mosque is the oldest mosque in the historic Sultanahmet district of Istanbul, and was the first imperial mosque in Ottoman times. The mosque has undergone several renovations since its construction in 1453. It

If you'd rather not look up the others, City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Center is a pretty good guess. Orr v. Orr might be as well since it is between 1985 and 1986.

A: []
A: No, Chlidanthus and Maianthemum are not the same genera, however Chlidanthus belongs to the same family as Maianthemum which is the family Asparagaceae. The family was previously divided into Asparagaceae sensu stricto and Ruscaceae, but molecular studies suggest that Ruscaceae is not a monophyletic group. Chlidanthus is currently treated as a separate genus within the Asparagaceae, though its classification within the family has been subject to much change.

A: No, Chlidanthus and Maianthemum are not the same genera, however Chlidanthus belongs to the same family as Maianthemum which is the family Asparagaceae. The family was previously divided into Asparagaceae sensu stricto and Ruscaceae, but molecular studies suggest that Ruscaceae is not a monophyletic group. A: []
A: No, Chlidanthus and Maianthemum are not the same genera, however Chlidanthus belongs to the same family as Maianthemum which is the family Asparagaceae. The family was previously divided into Asparagaceae sensu stricto and Ruscaceae, but molecular studies suggest that Ruscaceae is not a monophyletic group. Chlidanthus is currently treated as a separate genus within the Asparagaceae, though its classification within the family has been subject to much change. Chlidanthus includes one to two species, which are endemic to Mexico and South America. It is characterised by its pure white, fleshy, tubular flowers.

A: No, Chlidanthus and Maianthemum are not the same genera, however Chlidanthus belongs to the same family as Maianthemum which is the family Asparagaceae. The family was previously divided into Asparagaceae sensu stricto and Ruscaceae, but molecular studies suggest that Ruscaceae is not a monophyletic group. Chlidanthus is currently treated as a separate genus within the Asparagaceae, though its classification within the family has been subject to much change. Chlidanthus includes one to two species, which are endemic to Mexico and South America. It is characterised by its pure white, fleshy, tubular flowers. Maianthemum is commonly known as the starflower, but it does not have white, fleshy, tubular flowers, so the answer is no.

Q: Are Chlidanthus and Maianthemum both flowering plants?
A: "No, Chlidanthus and Maianthemum are not the same genera, however Chlidanthus belongs to the same family as Maianthemum which is the family Asparagaceae. While Chlidanthus itself is a monotypic genus of flowering plants, Maianthemum is a genus of flowering plants with about 40 species."

It is published by MIT, but it is independent of the Institute's internal activities and a not-for-profit organization. She is known for her mezzo-soprano vocal range. So the answer is: Tori Amos.

Which canal is located further North, Beaver and Erie Canal or the Dismal Swamp Canal?
A: []
A: The Beaver and Erie Canal is located further North in comparison to the Dismal Swamp Canal.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The Beaver and Erie Canal is located further North in comparison to the Dismal Swamp Canal. This is because the Dismal Swamp Canal is located in the state of North Carolina in the southern United States while the Beaver and Erie Canal is located in the northeastern United States and spanned the area between the New York Finger Lakes and the Great Lakes.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The Beaver and Erie Canal is located further North in comparison to the Dismal Swamp Canal. This is because the Dismal Swamp Canal is located in the state of North Carolina in the southern United States while the Beaver and Erie Canal is located in the northeastern United States and spanned the area between the New York Finger Lakes and the Great Lakes. While the Dismal Swamp Canal's counterpart is the Albemarle-Pamlico Canal, the counterpart to the Beaver and Erie Canal is the Erie Canal.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The Beaver and Erie Canal is located further North in comparison to the Dismal Swamp Canal. This is because the Dismal Swamp Canal is located in the state of North Carolina in the southern United States while the Beaver and Erie Canal is located in the northeastern United States and spanned the area between the New York Finger Lakes and the Great Lakes. While the Dismal Swamp Canal's counterpart is the Albemarle-Pamlico Canal, the counterpart to the Beaver and Erie Canal is the Erie Canal. So the answer is: The Beaver and Erie Canal (specifically, the Erie Canal).

A: The Beaver and Erie Canal is located further North in comparison to the Dismal Swamp Canal. Because Beaver and Erie canal is located in the state of New York, which is located in the North East of the USA, the Erie canal and the main line of the 374 mile long canal is not present as it's also 400 miles north of Philadelphia, but the Main Branch which starts in Rome in the Mohawk Valley and then runs 350 miles along the Mohawk Valley in the mountains between

5a83ed9b55429933447460b8
Cistus and Stapelianthus are both forms of what?
A: []
A: Cistus and Stapelianthus are both forms of cacti.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: Cistus and Stapelianthus are both forms of cacti. Some sources refer to them as cacti but the correct terminology is the plural form "cactuses".
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: Cistus and Stapelianthus are both forms of cacti. Some sources refer to them as cacti but the correct terminology is the plural form "cactuses". More specifically cistus is a real cactus genus, Stapelianthus is not a cactus but rather a euphorbiaceous plant genus.  
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: Cistus and Stapelianthus are both forms of cacti. Some sources refer to them as cacti but the correct terminology is the plural form "cactuses". More specifically cistus is a real cactus genus, Stapelianthus is not a cactus but rather a euphorbiaceous plant genus.   The term "cistus" refers to a group of about 20 species of flowering plants in the family Cistaceae.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: Cistus and Stapelianthus are both forms of cacti. Some sources refer to them as cacti but the correct terminology is the plural form "cactuses". More specifically cistus is a real cactus genus, Stapelianthus is not a cactus but rather a euphorbiaceous plant genus.   The term "cistus" refers to a group of about 20 species of flowering plants in the family Cistaceae. Some Cistus species are thorny shrubs that grow as high as 3 meters (10 ft), while other species are creeping or subshrubs with a maximum height of around 30 centimeters (12 in).

A: Cistus and Stapelianthus are both forms of cacti. Some sources refer to them as cacti but the correct terminology is the plural form "cactuses". More specifically cistus is a real cactus genus, Stapelianthus is not a cactus but rather a euphorbiaceous plant genus.   The term "cistus" refers to a group of about 20 species of flowering plants in the family Cistaceae. Some Cistus species are thorny shrubs that grow as high as 3 meters (10 ft), while other species are creeping or subshrubs with a maximum height of around 30 centimeters (12 in). In terms of morphological characteristics, the Cistus plants resemble the cacti.

A: Cistus and Stapelianthus are both forms of cacti. Some sources refer to them as cacti but the correct terminology is the plural form "cactuses". More specifically cistus is a real cactus genus, Stapelianthus is not a cactus but rather a euphorbiaceous plant genus.   The term "cistus" refers to a group of about 20 species of flowering plants in the family Cistaceae. Some Cistus species are thorny shrubs that grow as high as 3 meters (10 ft), while other species are creeping or subshrubs with a maximum height of around 30 centimeters (12 in). In terms of morphological characteristics, the Cistus plants resemble the cacti. So the answer is: cacti.

Not just a competitive game, Outrage! was designed to help players have good social bonding experiences with friends and family members. As a result of the simplicity of the game's mechanics, many of the players won't feel tired of learning it quickly. Though the game doesn't really concentrate on competition, the addition of a time limit for players to collect as many words as they can turns it into a fast-paced game.

A: []
A: The Carondelet Canal was completed and began operation first.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The Carondelet Canal was completed and began operation first. It operated from 1822 until 1849.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The Carondelet Canal was completed and began operation first. It operated from 1822 until 1849. The Miami and Erie Canal began operation in 1845, but due to engineering failures, it ceased operations after its initial opening in 1845 and only reopened for brief periods before ceasing operations permanently in 1909.
A: Exit? No.
A: []
A: The Carondelet Canal was completed and began operation first. It operated from 1822 until 1849. The Miami and Erie Canal began operation in 1845, but due to engineering failures, it ceased operations after its initial opening in 1845 and only reopened for brief periods before ceasing operations permanently in 1909. So the answer is: Carondelet Canal.
